The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the many years behind — more years remaining to the jubilee; the the greater price — proportioned to the longer service owed; the out of the money — reckoned from the sum he was bought for; the exact proportion — the price measured against the years recovered.

The witnesses agree the proportion was exact: the more service a man still owed before the jubilee would have freed him, the larger the price of his early release, for the buyer gave up the more; the law measured the redemption money precisely against the value of the years recovered, that the freedom bought be paid for justly and the owner fully answered.

So the witnesses make the verse the larger price: The witnesses note the many years behind: more years remaining to the jubilee. The witnesses note the the greater price: proportioned to the longer service owed. The witnesses note the out of the money: reckoned from the sum he was bought for. The witnesses note the exact proportion: the price measured against the years recovered. The expositors observe the many years behind: more years remaining to the jubilee. The expositors observe the the greater price: proportioned to the longer service owed. The expositors observe the out of the money: reckoned from the sum he was bought for. The expositors observe the exact proportion: the price measured against the years recovered. The believer is taught to mark the many years behind, the the greater price, the out of the money, the exact proportion, and to remember the LORD who redeems. The verse thus records that where many years remained, the redemption price was the greater — measured exactly against the service recovered, the owner justly answered.
52:
"And if there remain but few years unto the year of jubile, then he shall count with him, and according unto his years shall he give him again the price of his redemption." The witnesses note the rule when few years remain — the redemption price is the less, for the service owed is short. The witnesses note that Gill gives the example: if he has served fifteen years and but five remain, the less is given for his redemption. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the price counted by the years, the buyer keeping him meanwhile as a hired servant. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the few years behind — little service remaining to the jubilee; the count with him — the price reckoned by the years; the the lesser price — freedom cheaper as the jubilee nears; the time toward liberty — every year lightening the cost of release.

The witnesses agree the falling price as the jubilee neared showed the law's steady aim at liberty: every passing year lightened the cost of freedom, so that release grew ever easier and the jubilee at last set free for nothing; the structure itself leaned toward the deliverance of the captive, time working on the side of his redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the lesser price: The witnesses note the few years behind: little service remaining to the jubilee. The witnesses note the count with him: the price reckoned by the years. The witnesses note the the lesser price: freedom cheaper as the jubilee nears. The witnesses note the time toward liberty: every year lightening the cost of release. The expositors observe the few years behind: little service remaining to the jubilee. The expositors observe the count with him: the price reckoned by the years. The expositors observe the the lesser price: freedom cheaper as the jubilee nears. The expositors observe the time toward liberty: every year lightening the cost of release. The believer is taught to mark the few years behind, the count with him, the the lesser price, the time toward liberty, and to remember the LORD who redeems. The verse thus records that where few years remained, the redemption price was the less — the structure leaning toward liberty, time working on the side of the captive's release.
53:
"And as a yearly hired servant shall he be with him: and the other shall not rule with rigour over him in thy sight." The witnesses note that even in a stranger's house the brother was to be treated as a hired servant, not ruled with rigour. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was redeemable every year, and to be valued and treated as a hireling, not a bondman. The witnesses note that Ellicott presses that as long as he served, the master might not treat him like a slave. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a yearly hired servant — treated as a hireling, not a bondman; the redeemable every year — his freedom always within reach; the not with rigour — the master forbidden harsh rule; the in thy sight — the people charged to watch over their brother.

The witnesses agree Israel kept a watch over its own even in a foreigner's service: the brother sold to a stranger was still to be used as a free labourer, and the phrase "in thy sight" laid on the people a duty of oversight, that they see to it the heathen master deal gently with their brother — the covenant community answerable for the kindly treatment of every one of its own.

So the witnesses make the verse gently treated: The witnesses note the a yearly hired servant: treated as a hireling, not a bondman. The witnesses note the redeemable every year: his freedom always within reach. The witnesses note the not with rigour: the master forbidden harsh rule. The witnesses note the in thy sight: the people charged to watch over their brother. The expositors observe the a yearly hired servant: treated as a hireling, not a bondman. The expositors observe the redeemable every year: his freedom always within reach. The expositors observe the not with rigour: the master forbidden harsh rule. The expositors observe the in thy sight: the people charged to watch over their brother. The believer is taught to mark the a yearly hired servant, the redeemable every year, the not with rigour, the in thy sight, and to remember the LORD who redeems. The verse thus records that even in a stranger's house the brother was used as a hired man, not with rigour — the covenant community answerable to watch over the treatment of its own.
54:
"And if he be not redeemed in these years, then he shall go out in the year of jubile, both he, and his children with him." The witnesses note the last safeguard — if not redeemed before, the brother and his children went free at the jubilee. The witnesses note that Gill notes the text may be filled "in these years" or "by these" means — that is, by his kin or himself; if neither, the jubilee freed him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the brother going out at the jubilee even from a stranger's house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not redeemed before — no kin nor self having freed him; the go out in the jubilee — the certain deliverance at last; the he and his children — his family freed with him; the liberty reaching all — the jubilee breaking every bondage.

The witnesses agree the jubilee was the final and certain deliverance: though no kinsman redeemed him and he could not redeem himself, the brother could not be held past the jubilee, but went free with his children even out of the foreigner's house; the appointed year of liberty reached into every bondage and broke it, that no son of the covenant remain a slave.

So the witnesses make the verse the final safeguard: The witnesses note the not redeemed before: no kin nor self having freed him. The witnesses note the go out in the jubilee: the certain deliverance at last. The witnesses note the he and his children: his family freed with him. The witnesses note the liberty reaching all: the jubilee breaking every bondage. The expositors observe the not redeemed before: no kin nor self having freed him. The expositors observe the go out in the jubilee: the certain deliverance at last. The expositors observe the he and his children: his family freed with him. The expositors observe the liberty reaching all: the jubilee breaking every bondage. The believer is taught to mark the not redeemed before, the go out in the jubilee, the he and his children, the liberty reaching all, and to remember the LORD who redeems. The verse thus records that if none redeemed him, the jubilee freed the brother and his children even from a stranger's house — the appointed year of liberty reaching into every bondage.
55:
"For unto me the children of Israel are servants; they are my servants whom I brought forth out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note the closing ground of the whole law of servitude — Israel are God's servants, redeemed out of Egypt, and so not to be perpetual servants to men. The witnesses note that Gill draws the gospel parallel: as those redeemed by the blood of Christ should not be perpetual servants of men, so Israel, God's servants, were not to be enslaved to others. The witnesses note that, redeemed from Egypt, they were laid under obligation to serve the LORD, and he would not have others rule over them with rigour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the unto me are servants — israel God's own by redemption; the brought out of Egypt — freed from the house of bondage; the not servants to men — not to be enslaved, belonging to God; the the gospel parallel — the redeemed of Christ bought with a price (1 Cor. 7:23).

The witnesses agree the verse seals the chapter's great theme: Israel belonged to God by redemption, and therefore belonged to no man as a slave; Gill applies it to the church — the redeemed of Christ are bought with a price and are not to be the servants of men (1 Cor. 7:23), serving from the heart the Lord who set them free.

So the witnesses make the verse God's servants alone: The witnesses note the unto me are servants: Israel God's own by redemption. The witnesses note the brought out of Egypt: freed from the house of bondage. The witnesses note the not servants to men: not to be enslaved, belonging to God. The witnesses note the the gospel parallel: the redeemed of Christ bought with a price (1 Cor. 7:23). The expositors observe the unto me are servants: Israel God's own by redemption. The expositors observe the brought out of Egypt: freed from the house of bondage. The expositors observe the not servants to men: not to be enslaved, belonging to God. The expositors observe the the gospel parallel: the redeemed of Christ bought with a price (1 Cor. 7:23). The believer is taught to mark the unto me are servants, the brought out of Egypt, the not servants to men, the the gospel parallel, and to remember the LORD who redeems. The verse thus records that Israel were God's servants by redemption and so not to be enslaved to men — the redeemed of Christ likewise bought with a price, serving from the heart the Lord who freed them.
